content.text: The virtual DOM is React's central abstraction for describing what the UI should look like. Instead of writing directly to the browser's DOM — an expensive, stateful structure — React builds a lightweight JavaScript object tree that mirrors the desired UI, then figures out the minimal set of real DOM operations needed to bring the browser in sync.

attrs.alt: React render cycle from component to real DOM

content.text: A virtual DOM node is just a plain JavaScript object. It is cheap to create and throw away, carries no references to live browser APIs, and can be compared against another such object with simple property equality checks. That cheapness is the point: React can build a new tree on every render without worrying about performance, then do the expensive DOM work only once for the differences it finds.
From JSX to a Virtual DOM Node
When Babel compiles JSX, each element becomes a call to
. This function returns a plain object — the virtual DOM node — with four relevant fields:
Since React 17, Babel uses the automatic JSX transform (
. The output shape is the same — you no longer need to import React to use JSX.
String (DOM tag) or function/class (component). Determines what React renders.
Hint for the reconciler to match elements across renders. Defaults to null.
Escape hatch to access the underlying DOM node or component instance.
All other attributes, plus children. Frozen in development builds.
Symbol preventing JSON-injected objects from being treated as React elements.
React 16 replaced the original recursive vDOM with the Fiber architecture. Each node in the virtual DOM now corresponds to a

— a JavaScript object that also stores scheduling priority, work-in-progress state, and pointers to its parent, child, and sibling Fibers. The conceptual model remains the same: a tree of objects describing the UI.
The fields that matter for performance understanding are:
— same as the element type above
— props being processed vs props from the last completed render
— head of the hooks linked list for function components
— bitmask indicating which DOM effects are needed (Placement, Update, Deletion)
— pointer to the corresponding Fiber in the other tree (current ↔ work-in-progress)
React maintains two Fiber trees in memory: the
tree (what the browser reflects right now) and the
tree (what is being computed for the next commit). Completing a render swaps them — the work-in-progress becomes current.

React maintains:

  current tree ─────────────── what the browser shows now
       │
       │ each fiber has .alternate pointing to ──►
       │
  work-in-progress tree ─────── being built for the next render

  After commit: pointers swap. work-in-progress becomes current.
is where React builds the work-in-progress tree. It is pure and interruptible — React can pause and resume it to keep the browser responsive (concurrent mode). No DOM mutations happen here.
Never read from the DOM or trigger side-effects during render. The render phase may be interrupted in concurrent mode, making such reads unreliable.
applies the changes collected during render. It runs to completion without interruption and has three sub-phases: before mutation (
Insert, update, delete DOM nodes; set refs to null

State updates inside an event handler are batched — React waits until all handlers in the same event have run, then builds one new tree and performs one commit. React 18 extends this to async callbacks, fetch handlers, and timeouts via automatic batching.
After building the new tree, React walks both trees simultaneously and classifies each Fiber node into one of three effects:
— a new Fiber with no current counterpart; React creates a DOM node and inserts it
— same type but different props; React calls the relevant DOM API (e.g.
— a Fiber present in current but absent in work-in-progress; React removes the DOM node and runs cleanup effects
The diffing algorithm uses two heuristics that bound its complexity to O(n) instead of the theoretical O(n³) for comparing two trees:
), React tears down the entire subtree and builds a new one. Changing a component type always unmounts and remounts.
within a list, React matches children by
first. Elements with the same key are assumed to represent the same logical item across renders and are updated in place; elements whose key disappears are deleted.

Without keys, React falls back to positional matching. Adding or removing items at the start of a list causes every subsequent element to be re-rendered — even if nothing about those elements changed. Keys let React identify moved nodes and reuse their DOM instances.
A common misconception: a component re-rendering means it updates the DOM. It does not — it only builds a new virtual DOM node. The DOM update happens only if that node
from the corresponding node in the current tree. Cheap re-renders are fine; excessive DOM mutations are not.
Optimise re-renders only when profiling shows a problem. Premature memoisation adds complexity without measurable benefit in most applications.

React's DevTools Profiler shows you which components rendered, what triggered them, and how much time each render took. It does not show DOM mutations directly, but a component with a large rendered output that diffs with many changes will appear slower in the Profiler.
Use the React DevTools browser extension's Profiler tab for a visual flame graph. It highlights components that render frequently or take disproportionate time.
The virtual DOM model has costs:
Every render allocates new JavaScript objects — garbage collector pressure grows with tree size and render frequency
The diff walk is O(n) with small constants but is not free; very large trees (thousands of nodes) show measurable reconciliation time
For WebGL, Canvas, or SVG animations, React's model adds overhead that bypassing it entirely avoids

Frameworks like Svelte and Solid compile away the virtual DOM — they generate imperative DOM code at build time and update only the exact nodes that depend on each reactive variable. For highly dynamic lists or canvas-heavy UIs this can be faster. React's model trades some runtime overhead for a simpler programming model: you describe the final state, not the mutations.
Direct reactive assignments, no runtime diff
Signals update only dependent DOM nodes
The virtual DOM is a plain JavaScript object tree that lets React decouple

. Every render produces a new tree; React diffs it against the previous one and commits only the differences. The Fiber architecture underlying this since React 16 adds scheduling and interruption on top of the same conceptual model. Understanding the two phases — render (pure, interruptible) and commit (synchronous, effectful) — is the foundation for reasoning about performance and side-effect timing in React applications.

by design — once created, their props and children cannot change. To update the UI you create a new element tree. This immutability makes time-travel debugging and server-side snapshot testing straightforward: you can capture the exact virtual DOM at any point in time and compare it against any other.
(plain objects describing what to render) from
(functions or classes that produce elements). Elements are the output; components are the factory. A tree of elements can contain both DOM tags (strings like
A React element is never a DOM node. It is a description. React reads that description and decides when and how to update the DOM.

) render a subtree into a different DOM node, but they still exist within the virtual DOM tree. Events bubble through the virtual DOM hierarchy (not the real DOM one) — clicks inside a portal reach the React ancestor, not the physical DOM parent.
), React renders the virtual DOM to an HTML string without a browser DOM.
then takes that HTML and attaches React's event system and state to it on the client without re-creating the DOM nodes — a process called
. The client-side virtual DOM must match the server HTML exactly; mismatches trigger a warning and fall back to a full client render.
Concurrent Mode and the Render Phase

In React 18's concurrent mode, the render phase can be interrupted and restarted. If a higher-priority update (e.g. a user keystroke) arrives while rendering a low-priority tree, React discards the in-progress work-in-progress tree and starts fresh with the new state. This is safe precisely because the render phase is pure — no DOM mutations have happened yet.

Because the render phase is pure, React can call your component function multiple times before committing. If you put console logs or analytics calls inside the render function body, they may fire more than once in development (StrictMode deliberately double-invokes renders to surface such bugs).
Keys are the most common source of virtual DOM correctness bugs:
safe only when the list never reorders or gains items in the middle. Causes stale state when items move because React reuses the component instance for the new position.
produces a new key every render, forcing React to unmount and remount the component on every update — the most expensive possible operation.
two siblings with the same key cause unpredictable reconciliation. React will warn in development.
is valid and necessary when the fragment is a list item.
Wasted Renders and When to Optimise

When a parent re-renders, all its children re-render too — even children whose props did not change. This is by design: React cannot know whether a child's output will differ without calling it. The virtual DOM diff then finds nothing to commit for those children, so no DOM work happens. The cost is only the JavaScript execution of calling the child function.
Measure before optimising. Open the Profiler, interact with the UI, and look for components with high
that render on every parent update. Memoising a fast component saves less than the overhead of the memo check itself.
For very long lists (hundreds of visible items), virtualising the list avoids rendering off-screen elements at all. Libraries like
render only the items currently in the viewport, dramatically reducing both the virtual DOM size and the commit work:
Use list virtualisation when the list is longer than ~200 items and items are similar in structure. For heterogeneous (variable-height) lists, use
The practical takeaway: write components that describe the desired UI state, let React handle the DOM, and reach for
, or structural changes only when the Profiler identifies a concrete bottleneck.
